claim: The widely-circulated 'Short Pendulum Serve Tutorial | Backspin' (April 12, 2020, ~26.6k views) walks through grip, stance, stroke and how to generate backspin specifically for the short pendulum — the most directly on-topic free YouTube tutorial for this exact serve.
quote: Short Pendulum Serve Tutorial | Backspin ... "viewCount":"26584" ... "publishDate":"2020-04-12T15:17:11-07:00" ... how to do the short backspin pendulum serve. I cover how to hold the racket for this serve, the stance, the stroke and how to get the backspin.

claim: The 'Pendulum Serve Tutorial | Pure Backspin' follow-up (December 13, 2020, ~12.3k views) drills the harder pure-backspin variant in which the bat is parallel to the ground at contact, removing the residual sidespin many beginners produce by accident.
quote: Pendulum Serve Tutorial | Pure Backspin ... "viewCount":"12276" ... "publishDate":"2020-12-13T14:52:21-08:00" ... if you have your bat parallel to the ground you will get pure backspin.

claim: PingSkills' official 'Pendulum Serve Preview' (Alois Rosario / Jeff Plumb, ~55.4k views, 2015) is the canonical reference clip from the most-cited English-speaking coaching duo, and points to their full grip-stance-start-finish breakdown.
quote: Pendulum Serve Preview | PingSkills | Table Tennis ... "channelName":"PingSkills" ... "viewCount":"55388" ... "publishDate":"2015-12-29T16:17:50-08:00" ... we teach you how to do the reverse pendulum serve in table tennis.

claim: PingSkills' first-step drill: drop three lower fingers off the handle into a fist, grip with thumb and index only, slide the index further onto the backhand rubber — a grip change that frees the wrist and is the prerequisite for any spinny pendulum.
quote: Removing your three lower fingers from the bat handle ... Making a fist with those fingers behind the handle ... Gripping only with your thumb and index finger ... This allows you to use your wrist more freely.

claim: Tom Lodziak's same-motion pair drill — open bat angle and brush under the ball for backspin, then keep the same motion but straighten the bat angle at contact for no-spin — is the practical disguise drill the deep guide hinges on.
quote: for a backspin serve you should keep your bat angle more open to brush under the ball ... for the no spin serve, you use exactly the same service motion, but just straighten the bat angle at the last moment

claim: Tom Lodziak's 'Sidespin (pendulum) serve - tips and tactics' (~502k views, 2017) is his highest-viewed pendulum video, covering both backspin and topspin variants and how to set up the 3rd-ball attack.
quote: Sidespin (pendulum) serve - tips and tactics ... "channelName":"Tom Lodziak" ... "viewCount":"502472" ... I show you how to do a forehand sidespin serve (also known as a pendulum serve) ... how to vary spin and how to use the serve to set up a 3rd ball attack.

claim: TableTennisDaily's '5 Steps To Master The Reverse Pendulum Serve' (Dan Ives, ~798k views, 2019-10-23) is the highest-production-value step-by-step guide on YouTube and is the standard pair to their backspin ghost-serve video.
quote: 5 Steps To Master The Reverse Pendulum Serve | Table Tennis ... "viewCount":"797879" ... "publishDate":"2019-10-23T09:39:05-07:00" ... we've created this step by step guide so you can master it too!

claim: TableTennisDaily's '3 Steps To Master The Backspin GHOST SERVE' (~2.29M views, 2019) is the most-watched pure-backspin serve tutorial on YouTube — the canonical short-backspin variant that pairs naturally with a pendulum sidespin/backspin set.
quote: 3 Steps To Master The Backspin GHOST SERVE | Table Tennis ... "viewCount":"2289365" ... "publishDate":"2019-05-24T09:25:07-07:00"

claim: Larry Hodges (USATT Hall of Fame, USATT Certified National Coach) prescribes the off-table-to-on-table progression the YouTube tutorials assume: toss-and-spin off-table first, contact near the very bottom of the ball ~6 inches up, first bounce near the net to keep it short.
quote: Toss the ball ... spin the bottom of the ball with a right-to-left motion ... contact point near the ball's very bottom ... about six inches high ... first bounce near the net

claim: Coach EmRatThich (PingSunday) keys the short pendulum backspin on a loose grip with bat tilted slightly inward, brushing the lower side of the ball, body low and angled — and prescribes starting slow/spinny before adding speed, with a legal ~16 cm vertical toss.
quote: brush the ball's lower side. Use a loose grip and tilt the racket slightly inward ... knees bent and body low ... Start with slow, spinny serves to build consistency ... toss the ball straight up with a consistent height (around 16 cm as per ITTF rules)

claim: Tom Lodziak's central cue is to contact the ball more towards the handle where the bat is moving slower, using a similar action to the heavy-spin serve but without whipping the ball, and to keep the no-spin variant the same length and speed as the spin twin.
quote: contact the ball more towards the handle, where the bat is moving slower… use a similar action to your heavy spin serve. But don't whip the ball as much… a similar length and speed as your heavy spin serve

claim: Tom Lodziak's '4 simple serves for beginner players' video demonstrates backspin and no-spin together by changing bat angle at the last moment while keeping the same service motion.
quote: how to do four simple serves… a backspin serve, a topspin serve, a sidespin serve and a no spin serve… learn how to add different spins to the ball by changing your bat angle and swing trajectory

claim: PingSkills (Alois Rosario / Jeff Plumb) has a dedicated 'Backspin and No Spin Serve Variations' tutorial built around using the same motion to get attacking opportunities on the 3rd ball.
quote: get the backspin and no spin serve variations working for you… with an effective variation you will get a lot of attacking opportunities on the 3rd ball

claim: PingSkills' explicit cue is to contact the ball close to the handle of the blade because that part has moved through a smaller arc and so imparts less spin.
quote: you can contact the ball close to the handle of your blade, since that part of your blade has moved through a smaller arc and so you will impart less spin

claim: EmRatThich (PingSunday) frames deception as 'same motion, different contact point' and recommends using the same serve in different ways and different serves with the same action as the core training principle.
quote: change the contact point but keep the same serve motion, so with the same motion you can change the spin of your serve… train to use the same serve in different ways and different serves with the same action

claim: MLFM's 'Short Pendulum Serve Tutorial | No Spin' (uploaded 28 June 2020) is dedicated to the dead-ball variant and explicitly tells viewers to combine it with the backspin serve as the same-motion pair.
quote: I teach the dead ball serve in this video. Some tips to help with your dead ball serve include:* Contact the ball low* Combine with the backspin serve for be...

claim: Community consensus on float-serve deception adds two practical cues beyond the contact-point rule: tap flat instead of brushing, and stomp the foot at contact to mask the softer ball-blade sound.
quote: contact the ball as close to the handle as possible when going for a float serve, and stomping your foot during the serve helps suppress the sound of the ball contacting the bat

claim: Tom Lodziak's 'Trick your opponents with service deception' video (with coach Craig Bryant) covers backspin/topspin/sidespin deception explicitly built around making the action look the same but produce opposite spin.
quote: your service action looks like topspin, but the spin is actually backspin… backspin deception, topspin deception and sidespin deception

claim: TableTennisDaily's 'FOOL Your Opponents: Learn to Change the Spin of Your Pendulum Table Tennis Serves' (4-min edit, 1 May 2025) is the most current dedicated tutorial on switching pendulum spin between backspin/sidespin/topspin/no-spin from one motion.
quote: In this 4 minute edit, learn how to change between backspin, sidespin, topspin and no spin variations for the pendulum table tennis serve.

claim: TableTennisDaily's longer 'Changing Spin on the Pendulum Serve' seminar (Mar 2020) explicitly teaches keeping the service action visually identical while swapping spins — closest match to the same-motion drill the parent needs.
quote: a short seminar-style lesson on how to change between backspin, sidespin and topspin on the pendulum serve - all while maintaining a service action...

claim: MLFM's 'Learning UNPREDICTABLE Backspin & No Spin Serve' (29 Nov 2019) is the explicit pair-tutorial covering both serves together as a deception package.
quote: Learning UNPREDICTABLE Backspin & No Spin Serve | MLFM Table Tennis Tutorial

claim: MLFM's 'FAST Pendulum Serve Tutorial | No Spin' (17 May 2020) cues mimicking the spin-serve motion at higher pace as the deception layer for the long no-spin variant.
quote: I teach you how to do the fast no spin pendulum serve in this video. My top tips for doing the fast no-spin are to:* Contact the ball low* Try to mimic your...

claim: PingSkills 'How To Serve Fast And Long' (Alois Rosario, 14 years old, ~1.6M views, 2:50) is the most-watched short tutorial on the long fast serve.
quote: How To Serve Fast And Long | Table Tennis | Pingskills ... PingSkills 258K abonnees ... 1,6 mln. weergaven 14 jaar geleden

claim: Tom Lodziak's 'How to do a SUPER FAST long serve (with Paul Drinkhall)' (May 2019, 5:57, ~211K views) features pro Paul Drinkhall demonstrating the serve as a 3rd-ball-attack setup.
quote: How to do a SUPER FAST long serve (with Paul Drinkhall) ... 211K weergaven 6 jaar geleden ... pro player Paul Drinkhall, shows how to do his super fast long serve ... set up a strong 3rd ball attack

claim: Craig Bryant's '4 Fast & Furious Long Serves | Outsmart Your Opponents' (~1 year old, 88K views, 14:48) is the dedicated long-fast-serve deep-dive on his TT Service Guy channel, covering four variations to push opponents back.
quote: 4 Fast & Furious Long Serves | Outsmart Your Opponents ... 88K weergaven 1 jaar geleden ... Craig Bryant breaks down four variations of long, fast table tennis serves designed to push opponents back ... maximizing speed, using spin as a weapon, and creating deceptive skidding effects

claim: Lodziak x Craig Bryant '4 killer serves to destroy your opponents' (~7 years old, 9.2M views, 11:37) explicitly includes the 'fast down the line' as one of four killer serves and is the most-viewed serve tutorial on Lodziak's channel.
quote: 4 killer serves to destroy your opponents (with Craig Bryant) ... 9,2 mln. weergaven 7 jaar geleden ... The serves in this video include: the kicker, fast down the line, super wide sidespin, and the ghost serve

claim: Lodziak's checklist for an effective long serve is fast, low over the net, bouncing close to opponent's baseline, placed in a corner or to the elbow, and disguised — with the first bounce on your side 'very close to your end of the table'.
quote: be fast ... go low over the net ... bounce close to your opponent's baseline ... be placed in a corner or to a player's elbow ... be disguised ... first bounce on your side must be very close to your end of the table

claim: Lodziak's frequency rule: occasional long serves to surprise active opponents, but up to 80% long serves vs passive players who don't attack — directly justifying the 2-3-per-game ambush usage at club level.
quote: you can use long serves a lot, as much as 80% of the time ... opponents who can attack with enough power that you cannot return the ball ... revert to mostly short/half-long serves

claim: Expert Table Tennis (Ben Larcombe) gives the two non-negotiables for fast serves: contact the ball low (let it drop near net/table height) and aim the first bounce at your own endline corner so the trajectory stays flat and forward.
quote: let the ball drop much further than feels comfortable ... aim for your own endline ... bounce as close to your corner as possible ... hit the ball more forwards instead of down

claim: Learn Table Tennis 'How to Perform a SUPER FAST SERVE?' (Mar 2025, ~181K views, 0:06+) is a recent short-form alternative covering speed, trajectory and spin factors.
quote: How to Perform a SUPER FAST SERVE? - Table Tennis Fast Serve Tutorial ... Learn Table Tennis ... 181K weergaven 1 jaar geleden ... key factors that influence speed, trajectory, and spin

claim: Lodziak's drill collection prescribes specific elbow-targeted serve drills: '3rd Ball Drill 2' has the attacker serve half-long backspin to the feeder's elbow forcing a weak return, and 'Receive Drill 2' practises returning long serves to the backhand or elbow.
quote: 3rd Ball Drill 2: Backspin Serve, Backhand Topspin ... attacker serves half-long backspin to the feeder's elbow ... Receive Drill 2: Backhand or Elbow ... feeder serves long sidespin to either the attacker's backhand or elbow

claim: Craig Bryant runs 'The Table Tennis Service Guy' channel (~80K subs) which is exclusively service-focused, making it the highest-density single source for serve-specific drills including long-fast variations.
quote: Craig Bryant - The Table Tennis Service Guy ... 79,6K abonnees ... A table tennis channel dedicated to improving your service game

claim: Lodziak's site page for the Drinkhall fast long serve confirms the tactical framing: this serve is used to 'catch his opponent out' rather than as a default, supporting the 2-3-per-game ambush thesis.
quote: Paul uses this serve quite often when playing matches to catch his opponent out and set up a strong attacking opportunity on the third ball

claim: Dimitrij Ovtcharov's '4 Steps to learn the World's Best Tomahawk Serve' (Jan 2022) is a top-tier tutorial from a world-class pro who relies on this serve in match play.
quote: 4 Steps with Dimitrij Ovtcharov to learn the Worlds Best Tomahawk Serve (published January 11, 2022)

claim: PingSkills' 'Low Tomahawk Serve' video with coach Alois Rosario teaches keeping the ball low by getting the body down and contacting from below, which is essential for a short tomahawk.
quote: Low Tomahawk Serve | PingSkills | Table Tennis - YouTube ... A PingSkills video posted in February 2012

claim: PingSkills' Alois Rosario emphasises that the tomahawk action 'comes naturally to a lot of people' and that small bat-angle changes disguise spin variations on the same motion.
quote: the action comes naturally to a lot of people ... small changes of your bat angle ... you get low and contact the ball from lower

claim: Adam Bobrow's UTT 'Tomahawk Serve Tutorial' (Aug 2019) is a widely cited entertainment-flavoured tutorial that walks through the throwing-axe motion for amateurs.
quote: Demo Zone segment at UTT (Ultimate Table Tennis, the Indian premier league for table tennis). The video was published on August 8, 2019

claim: TableTennisDaily's 'Forehand Tomahawk Serve | Topspin' (May 2020) frames the serve as 'the axe serve' and stresses brushing up the back of the ball after getting low, ideal for a short side-topspin variant.
quote: the tomahawk serve (also called the axe serve) is a great serve that is easy to load up with side-topspin ... published on May 10, 2020

claim: TableTennisDaily's 'The EASIEST Serve to Learn - Tomahawk Tutorial' (Sep 2024) is the most recent mainstream beginner-friendly tutorial, pitching tomahawk as the lowest-effort serve to add to a club player's arsenal.
quote: The Tomahawk Serve has been a popular serve for many years throughout many levels of play (tutorial from September 1, 2024)

claim: William Henzell's tomahawk serve demo on ttEDGE (Mar 2015) shows a 3-time Olympian's slow, low, hard-to-attack short tomahawk - useful as a pro reference video for the motion.
quote: William Henzell is a 3-time Olympian who has become known for his distinctive tomahawk serve. He performs the tomahawk serve without putting a lot of speed on the ball, which makes it difficult to attack ... posted on YouTube on March 18, 2015

claim: Samson Dubina's 'The Forehand Tomahawk Serve' (Feb 2014) explains the right-to-left tip-up motion and explicitly recommends serving from the forehand side into the receiver's wide forehand for maximum break-away angle.
quote: done with the forehand, tip up, with a right-to-left motion, creating a spin that breaks to the left ... when done from the forehand side into the receiver's wide forehand, the motion allows maximum angle that really breaks into the receiver's wide forehand

claim: Expert Table Tennis describes the canonical tomahawk technique - lifting the elbow, extending the arm and 'imagining throwing an actual tomahawk' while contacting the upper-right rear of the ball and finishing under it - and recommends placing it short to the opponent's forehand 95% of the time.
quote: Lift your elbow and extend your arm outward, striking the ball with your forehand rubber ... imagining throwing an actual tomahawk ... contacting the ball at the upper right-hand corner of the ball from the rear ... serves 95% of the time to the opponent's forehand

claim: Larry Hodges (TableTennisCoaching.com) confirms the strategic value of a short tomahawk to the receiver's forehand: it produces 'tremendous sidespin that breaks away from the receiver,' forcing last-second lunges and errors.
quote: tremendous sidespin that breaks away from the receiver, frequently causing a last-second lunge as the receiver reaches for the ball, leading to many mistakes

claim: The 5-step pattern: (1) wrist-only feel square to the table, (2) add forearm side-on with elbow lifted, (3) layer waist/shoulder rotation, (4) vary contact point to switch between sidespin/backspin and sidespin/topspin, (5) drill solo.
quote: Cut under the ball more for backspin and brush around the side more to create more topspin... Bring in the forearm for stability and control... Elevate your elbow to create adequate space for the backswing.

claim: MLFM Table Tennis 'Learning KILLER Reverse Pendulum Serve' (Nov 2019, ~315k views, 8:30) is a popular alternative walkthrough emphasising the deadly-spin angle for amateurs.
quote: Learning KILLER Reverse Pendulum Serve | MLFM Table Tennis Tutorial

claim: PingSkills' 'Learn the Backspin Reverse Pendulum Serve' (~2.57M views) is a focused short on the hardest variation — getting genuine backspin out of the reverse motion.
quote: Learn the Backspin Reverse Pendulum Serve | Table Tennis | PingSkills

claim: PingSkills' classic 'Pendulum & Reverse Pendulum Serve' demo (Apr 2008, ~797k views) shows both serves side-by-side, useful as a visual contrast for a player upgrading from pendulum.
quote: Footage from our original Table Tennis DVD showing the pendulum serve and the reverse pendulum serve.

claim: Tom Lodziak hosts coach Tyla Anderson teaching the reverse pendulum (technique, grip, spin variation, service length and placement) plus a return-challenge segment with Tom.
quote: technique, grip, spin variation, service length and service placement... hugely effective but can be tricky to execute

claim: Ti Long Club's 'Zhang Jike's Reverse Pendulum Serve | Tutorial & Secret' (Jun 2021, ~160k views, 6:57) is a strong pro-player breakdown of the most-imitated reverse pendulum action in the modern game.
quote: Ti Long instructs Zhang Jike's Reverse Pendulum Serve technique to the world table tennis community. The most detailed instructions, the easiest to practice.

claim: PingSunday's 'Why Ma Long has very good pendulum serve' (Nov 2022, ~76k views, 5:36) explains why Ma Long's serve sets up his 3rd-ball attack — a strategic 'watch the pros' companion to the technique tutorials.
quote: Ma Long's pendulum serve is short, so it's hard to attack it strong. Because it has sidespin, when you push it, Ma Long will attack it right after the serve (3rd ball attack).

claim: EmRatThich (PingSunday) summarises the reverse pendulum as racket-moves-outside-inward generating right-to-left sidespin; spin direction varies by racket angle and contact point — heavily used by Zhang Jike.
quote: The racket moves from the outside of your body inward during contact... heavily used by professional players like Zhang Jike

claim: 'Zhang Jike Reverse Pendulum Serve Slow Motion' (Aug 2016, ~65k views, 5:06) is a pure slow-motion reference of the canonical pro action, useful for visual modelling without verbal instruction.
quote: Zhang Jike Reverse Pendulum Serve Slow Motion

claim: Inspire Table Tennis's 'Reverse Pendulum Serve Spin Guide [2 Spins, 1 motion]' (Feb 2026, 4:51) targets the deception layer — making backspin and topspin look identical from one motion.
quote: how to create sidespin backspin and sidespin topspin / upspin with 1 motion

claim: JOOLA's Matt Hetherington offers a competing 3-step text framework — rocking-step weight transfer, draw racket into a 'box' with wrist parallel to bicep, flick out of the box into contact — useful as a written supplement.
quote: flick out of the box shape with your wrist and into the ball

claim: PingSkills is run by Jeff Plumb (Sydney 2000 Olympian) and Alois Rosario, who has coached the Australian Olympic team and Paralympic teams from 2012 through 2024.
quote: Jeff Plumb won the qualification tournament for the Sydney 2000 Olympics... Alois Rosario's coaching journey has included guiding National Junior champions and Olympic athletes... at the 2012 London Paralympics, the 2016 Rio Paralympics, the 2020 Tokyo Paralympics, and the 2024 France Paralympics.

claim: PingSkills YouTube has approximately 200K subscribers, having earned the YouTube Silver Play Button.
quote: PingSkills - Table Tennis Tutorials 200K subs on YouTube

claim: Tom Lodziak is a Table Tennis England Level 2 coach based in Cambridge who has trained under Richard Prause (Timo Boll's former coach), Desmond Douglas and others; his YouTube has over 275,000 subscribers and 50 million views.
quote: Tom Lodziak is a Table Tennis England Level 2 coach based in Cambridge, England... received coaching from many top players and coaches including Richard Prause (Timo Boll's former coach), Rade Markovic, Desmond Douglas... popular YouTube channel with over 275,000 subscribers and 50 million video views.

claim: Tom Lodziak's serve content explicitly targets amateur and league players, with detailed pendulum/sidespin serve walkthroughs that distinguish sidespin-backspin and sidespin-topspin variations.
quote: Epic sidespin serve tutorial... how to do a long fast sidespin serve, also known as a pendulum serve. The serve has two variations – sidespin with backspin and sidespin with topspin

claim: TableTennisDaily founder Dan Ives is a former semi-professional player in France with a first-class honours degree in Sports Coaching & Performance and 15+ years of coaching experience.
quote: Dan Ives, with 15+ years of coaching experience, is the founder of TableTennisDaily... set up TableTennisDaily in 2010 when he was playing semi-professional table tennis in France... first-class honours' degree in Sports coaching and Performance

claim: TableTennisDaily Academy collaborator Andrew Baggaley is a multiple English national champion who reached a world ranking of No. 139 and was England No. 1.
quote: A multiple English national champion, Andrew Baggaley turned professional aged 13... Highest World Ranking of No. 139 and Highest England Ranking of No. 1

claim: PingSunday is run by Coach EmRatThich, a Vietnamese-born, France-based club coach (PhD from Sorbonne) who teaches via the Chinese coaching philosophy with a 250K-subscriber YouTube following.
quote: Coach EmRatThich was born in Hanoi, Vietnam in 1983... After finishing his Ph.D. in Paris, University Pierre Marie Curie (Sorbonne University) in 2011, he is now a table tennis coach in a small club in France... Using the Chinese coaching philosophy

claim: Adam Bobrow is the official ITTF/World Table Tennis commentator since 2014 and his main YouTube channel had ~1.43M subscribers and 429M views as of late 2025; his content skews entertainment with some technique demonstrations.
quote: In 2014, Bobrow won a competition to become the 'voice of table tennis', and has been the official commentator for the International Table Tennis Federation ever since... approximately 1.43 million subscribers with 429 million views

claim: Pongfinity is a Finnish entertainment-leaning trio (Emil, Otto, Miikka) with 4.67M YouTube subscribers — the largest table-tennis channel — though Otto and Miikka are semi-pro Finnish national team players, not coaches.
quote: Pongfinity has 4.67 million subscribers, and their YouTube channel is the 7th most-subscribed channel in Finland and the most-subscribed channel about table tennis... Both Otto and Miikka are semi-professional players, and both represented Finland during the European and World Table Tennis Championships

claim: Coach Brian Pace of Dynamic Table Tennis is a USATT Certified National Coach with a peak USATT rating of 2610, top-300 world ranking, three collegiate championships, and a 4-0 record coaching students to national titles.
quote: Brian is a USATT National Level Coach... 3-Time Collegiate Champions... peak USATT rating of 2610 and was ranked within the top 300 in the world... coached his students to National Titles with a record of 4-0

claim: ExpertTableTennis is run by Ben Larcombe, a UKCC Level 2 coach (~150th in England) best known for the viral 'Expert in a Year' project; his channel skews podcast/long-form analysis over high-frequency video tutorials.
quote: Ben Larcombe... is a professional table tennis coach. He started Expert Table Tennis back in 2012... UKCC level 2 table tennis coach... 'Expert in a Year' challenge... almost 3 million views

claim: Independent reviews place PingSkills, TableTennisDaily, Tom Lodziak and PingSunday/EmRatThich at the top of recommended coaching channels, with Pongfinity and Adam Bobrow filling the entertainment tier; PechPongTT (Seth Pech, USA national team) is rising as a tactical-analysis option.
quote: PingSkills | Beginner-focused technique instruction; straightforward explanations | Jeff Plumb (Sydney 2000 Olympian); Alois Rosario (World Championships)... PechPongTT | In-depth tactical and technical tutorials... Seth Pech - semi-professional American player; USA national team experience
